children.name: Erinaceus
children.description: True hedgehogs of Europe and Asia — iconic, spiny insectivores that have inhabited the Old World for around 15 million years. Their 5,000–7,000 hollow keratin spines are modified hairs, and when threatened a hedgehog can roll into a near-perfect ball by contracting the orbicularis muscle that runs around its back, protecting all exposed soft tissue. Hedgehogs are partial hibernators, entering torpor when temperatures drop and food becomes scarce. The Western European hedgehog has declined by roughly 50% in the UK since 2000, driven by agricultural intensification, habitat fragmentation, road mortality, and badger predation.

children.name: Erinaceus europaeus
children.description: Britain's only native spiny mammal. Declared a species of principal importance for conservation in England and Wales. A single animal roams up to 2 km per night in search of invertebrates. Particularly at risk from road collisions and slug pellets.

children.name: Erinaceus amurensis
children.description: Found in northeastern China, Korea, and the Russian Far East. Closely related to the European hedgehog; sometimes considered a subspecies. The easternmost of the true hedgehogs.

children.name: Erinaceus concolor
children.description: Found across the Balkans, Turkey, and the Middle East, distinguished from the European hedgehog by its white-tipped belly fur. Favours dry scrubland, olive groves, and gardens. Hybridises with the northern white-breasted hedgehog where their ranges meet.

children.name: Erinaceus roumanicus
children.description: Ranges from central Europe and the Baltic states east to western Russia and the Caucasus. Has white-tipped belly spines and a pale face; distinguished from E. europaeus by its white breast patch. Has expanded westwards in recent decades, coming into contact with native hedgehog populations.

children.name: Atelerix
children.description: African hedgehogs — four-toed hedgehogs of sub-Saharan Africa and the Sahara. The African pygmy hedgehog (Atelerix albiventris) has become a popular pet worldwide, particularly in North America and Japan, though ownership is restricted in some US states. African hedgehogs tend to be smaller and more arid-adapted than their European relatives; some can aestivate during dry season in addition to hibernating in cold seasons. The four-toed hedgehog lacks the first toe on its hind feet, distinguishing it from European species.

children.name: Atelerix albiventris
children.description: The most widely kept hedgehog species. Wild populations range across the savannah belt of sub-Saharan Africa. Tolerates a wider range of habitats than European species. Self-anointing behaviour — rubbing saliva onto its spines — is particularly pronounced in this species.

children.name: Atelerix algirus
children.description: Found across North Africa from Morocco to Libya, and introduced to the Canary Islands, the Balearic Islands, and Malta. Paler than the European hedgehog and more heat-tolerant. Often thrives in dry coastal scrub, gardens, and farmland around the Mediterranean.

children.name: Atelerix frontalis
children.description: Ranges from Zimbabwe and Mozambique south to the Western Cape of South Africa. A distinctive white facial band marks the forehead. Nocturnal and solitary; occupies scrubland, grassland, and suburban gardens. Listed as Least Concern but threatened by road kills and collection for traditional medicine.

children.name: Atelerix sclateri
children.description: Confined to Somalia and possibly adjacent parts of Ethiopia; one of the least-studied hedgehog species. Small and pale, adapted to semi-arid thornbush habitat. Rarely seen and data-deficient; named in honour of zoologist Philip Lutley Sclater.

children.name: Hylomys
children.description: Moonrats and gymnures — the most primitive members of the hedgehog family, lacking spines entirely. Moonrats superficially resemble large shrews or small opossums and represent the closest living relatives of true hedgehogs. The greater moonrat (Echinosorex gymnura) is the largest insectivore in Southeast Asia, reaching 40 cm in body length and smelling powerfully of ammonia. Gymnures are forest floor specialists of Southeast Asian rainforest; the lesser gymnure (Hylomys suillus) is one of the most abundant small mammals in the cloud forests of Borneo.

children.name: Hylomys suillus
children.description: A common but rarely seen insectivore of montane Southeast Asian forest. Lives among roots and leaf litter. Produces a musty, unpleasant scent from glands along its flanks.

children.name: Hylomys dorsalis
children.description: Restricted to the montane forests of Borneo, typically above 900 m elevation in cool mossy cloud forest. Distinguished from the lesser gymnure by a darker dorsal stripe and larger size. Known only from scattered museum specimens and camera-trap records.

children.name: Hemiechinus
children.description: Long-eared hedgehogs of Central and South Asia and the Middle East, distinguished by conspicuously large ears that aid heat dissipation in arid environments. They run faster than true hedgehogs and are less inclined to roll into a defensive ball, preferring to flee. Capable of short bursts of speed and are more agile climbers than European hedgehogs.

children.name: Hemiechinus auritus
children.description: The most widespread long-eared hedgehog, ranging from Egypt and Libya east through Arabia and Central Asia to Mongolia and northern China. Extremely hardy; tolerates temperatures from −20 °C in winter to over 40 °C in summer by entering torpor. Popular in the exotic pet trade despite being protected in many countries.

children.name: Hemiechinus collaris
children.description: Found in India and Pakistan, primarily in dry scrub forest and sandy desert habitats of the northwest. Paler than H. auritus and slightly smaller. Often seen in agricultural land on the edges of villages; feeds mainly on insects and small lizards.

children.name: Mesechinus
children.description: Steppe and montane hedgehogs of Central and East Asia, sometimes called Daurian hedgehogs. Adapted to cold, open grassland and steppe habitats from Mongolia to northeastern China. Smaller than Erinaceus but with similar habits; hibernate deeply in winter in burrows dug into stiff soil.

children.name: Mesechinus dauuricus
children.description: Found across the steppes and dry grasslands of Mongolia and northern China, with a small population in Russia's Transbaikalia. One of the most cold-tolerant hedgehogs; hibernates for up to six months. Feeds on insects, worms, and small vertebrates in open grassland and rocky slopes.

children.name: Paraechinus
children.description: Desert hedgehogs of North Africa, Arabia, and South Asia — the most arid-adapted members of the family. Capable of surviving in true desert conditions by aestivating during extreme heat and drought. Their larger ears and paler colouration reflect their desert lifestyle. Often eat scorpions and other prey that other hedgehogs avoid.

children.name: Paraechinus aethiopicus
children.description: Inhabits the Sahara Desert and Arabian Peninsula, one of the most arid-adapted of all hedgehogs. Remarkable immunity to scorpion venom allows it to eat large scorpions without ill effect. Pale creamy spines provide camouflage in sand, and it can survive without free water by metabolising moisture from prey.

children.name: Paraechinus micropus
children.description: Found in Pakistan and northwestern India in semi-arid scrub and dry rocky terrain. Distinctive for its dark face mask contrasting with pale spines. Feeds on invertebrates and small lizards; can survive periods of food scarcity by lowering its metabolic rate.

children.name: Echinosorex
children.description: Monotypic genus containing the greater moonrat, the largest insectivore in Asia and one of the most distinctive members of the hedgehog family. Unlike its relatives it bears no spines, instead covered in coarse black and white fur. Produces an intensely musky odour resembling ammonia, used for territorial marking along watercourses.

children.name: Echinosorex gymnura
children.description: The largest insectivore in Southeast Asia, reaching 40 cm in body length and 1.4 kg in weight. Found in lowland rainforest, mangroves, and riverine habitats in Sumatra, Borneo, and the Malay Peninsula. Nocturnal and semi-aquatic; excellent swimmer that hunts fish, earthworms, and crustaceans along forest streams.

children.name: Neotetracus
children.description: Monotypic genus of primitive insectivores from southern China and adjacent Southeast Asia. The shrew gymnure superficially resembles a large shrew with its elongated snout and slender build, but belongs firmly within Erinaceidae. Represents one of the oldest lineages within the family.

children.name: Neotetracus sinensis
children.description: Found in montane forests of southern China (Yunnan, Sichuan, Guizhou) at elevations of 1,000–3,000 m. Small, with a long pointed snout and dense brown fur, lacking the white facial markings of moonrats. Feeds on earthworms, insects, and plant matter in moist forest floor litter.
